John 3:16.commentary: The most-quoted verse in scripture. Notice three movements: God's love (motive), the gift of His Son (means), and everlasting life for the believer (result). 'World' here is humanity in rebellion — not a deserving recipient. 'Whosoever' is wide-open: no ethnic, moral, or social prerequisite. The condition isn't earning but believing — trusting Christ for what only He can do.

John 1:1.commentary: John deliberately echoes Genesis 1:1. The 'Word' (Greek: Logos) was a term Greek philosophers used for the rational principle ordering the universe — and that Jews used for God's creative speech. John says: this Word was God, was with God, and became flesh. The Trinity in seed form, the deity of Christ asserted in 17 words.

John 14:6.commentary: Jesus's most exclusive claim. Not 'a' way but 'the' way. The early disciples were called 'people of the Way' (Acts 9:2). This verse is one of the hardest in a pluralistic culture — and the church has always insisted it's non-negotiable, because Jesus said it.

Romans 8:28.commentary: Often quoted, often misunderstood. Paul does NOT say all things are good — he says all things work TOGETHER for good. The promise is restricted: 'to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose.' The 'good' here is defined in v.29: being conformed to the image of Christ. Not always comfort. Always character.

Philippians 4:13.commentary: Read v.11-12 first: Paul has just said he has learned to be content whether full or hungry, whether abounding or in need. Verse 13 isn't a promise that Christ will help you win the game or get the promotion. It's the secret of contentment in any circumstance — strength to endure, not strength to succeed on your terms.

Psalm 23:1.commentary: David — a shepherd before he was a king — knows the trade. A shepherd provides, protects, leads, restores. Jesus calls Himself the Good Shepherd in John 10, deliberately picking up Psalm 23. 'I shall not want' isn't 'I get everything I crave' — it's 'I lack nothing I truly need.'

Genesis 1:1.commentary: The Bible's first sentence makes four claims that shocked the ancient world: God exists before everything, He's singular (not a pantheon), the universe had a beginning (not eternal), and He made it (not random emergence). Every page after this rests on those four claims.

Romans 3:23.commentary: The leveling verse. There's no class of person — religious or irreligious, moral or immoral — that's exempt. Paul has spent two chapters showing both Gentile pagans and observant Jews are equally under sin. The verse demolishes any 'I'm not that bad' theology and prepares the way for justification by faith in v.24.

Romans 6:23.commentary: Two transactions in one verse. Sin pays wages — what you earn — and the wage is death. But God gives a gift — what you cannot earn — and the gift is eternal life in Christ Jesus. Notice the careful word choice: wages vs. gift. You don't earn salvation. You receive it.

Ephesians 2:8-9.commentary: The Reformation distilled into two verses. Three negatives ('not of yourselves… not of works… lest any man should boast') guard the gospel from any human contribution. Grace is the source. Faith is the channel. The whole package is the gift of God. If you contributed, you'd boast. You can't, so you won't.

Matthew 28:19-20.commentary: Called the Great Commission. The verb 'go' is actually participial — 'as you are going.' Discipleship happens in motion. Three actions: make disciples (the main verb), baptize, teach. The Trinity is named (Father, Son, Holy Ghost), and the promise of presence is the punctuation: 'I am with you always.'

Proverbs 3:5-6.commentary: Two negatives and three positives. Don't lean on your own understanding; trust God with ALL your heart. Acknowledge Him in ALL your ways. The promise: He will direct your paths. Notice how absolute the conditions are — 'all' twice. Half-trust gets half-direction.

Isaiah 53:5.commentary: Written ~700 years before the cross, this verse is the clearest pre-Christian description of substitutionary atonement. Five trades: He was wounded for OUR transgressions; bruised for OUR iniquities; chastised for OUR peace; striped for OUR healing. Every pronoun is exchanged.

Matthew 11:28.commentary: Open invitation. No qualifiers — not 'come if you've cleaned up,' not 'come if you have something to offer.' Come. Bring the labor and the heavy burdens. Jesus doesn't lift them off; He shoulders them with you (v.29 — 'take my yoke,' the harness for two oxen). Rest by re-pairing, not by escaping.

Psalm 46:10.commentary: The Hebrew is more forceful than 'be still' — literally 'let go,' 'cease striving.' The verse comes in the middle of a psalm about cosmic chaos: nations roaring, mountains falling into the sea. Even in that, the command is to stop frantic activity and recognize that God remains God.

Galatians 2:20.commentary: The Christian's identity in a single sentence. Crucified with Christ — my old self is dead. Yet I live — but it's no longer the old me running things. Christ lives in me. And my present existence in the flesh is lived 'by the faith of the Son of God' — His faith, His faithfulness, sustaining me.

Hebrews 11:1.commentary: The Bible's working definition of faith. Faith gives substance (Greek: hupostasis, 'standing-under') to hope, and evidence to things unseen. Not the absence of evidence — faith IS the evidence in scripture's logic. The whole chapter is examples of people who acted on the basis of unseen realities they trusted.

1 John 1:9.commentary: Two divine attributes guarantee the promise: God is faithful (He keeps His word) and just (the cross paid for these sins, so forgiving them is righteous, not merely merciful). Confession isn't groveling for fresh forgiveness; it's the believer's housekeeping in an already-secured relationship.

Jeremiah 29:11.commentary: Often quoted out of context — this was a letter to exiles in Babylon who would NOT see Jerusalem again. The 'expected end' was 70 years away. God's good plans don't always mean comfort soon. They sometimes mean faithfulness through long captivity. Still beautiful, but heavier than the coffee-mug version.
